metadata.format_notes: User messages are verbatim. Assistant responses are substantive summaries (actual responses were often long; full decisions captured in PROPOSAL.md, python-skill-script-style-requirements.md, deferred-items.md, and proposal-critique-v06-revision.md). Tool-use descriptions omitted; only conversational exchange captured. This pass was a defect critique with grounded research, surfacing integration gaps the v005→v006 language migration left in PROPOSAL.md and the Python style doc.

turns.user: read and follow  /data/projects/livespec/brainstorming/approach-2-nlspec-based/critique-interview-prompt.md
turns.assistant: Read the critique-interview-prompt.md. Resolved versioning knobs: N=006, NEXT=007, CRITIQUE=06. Loaded required context: PROPOSAL.md (v006), python-skill-script-style-requirements.md, livespec-nlspec-spec.md, history/README.md, v006 critique + revision + conversation.json, plus brainstorming companion docs (goals/non-goals, subdomains, prior-art, lifecycle diagrams, terminology summary) and v005 PROPOSAL.md for migration context. Produced the v007 critique at brainstorming/proposed_changes/v007-proposed-change-proposal-critique-v06.md with 18 items (G1-G18) labelled with the NLSpec failure-mode framework (ambiguity / malformation / incompleteness / incorrectness), grouped as major (G1-G5; recreatability-blocking), significant (G6-G12; load-bearing guesses), and smaller (G13-G18; wording fixes).

Asked G1 (Python import-path bootstrap): how does `from livespec.commands.seed import main` resolve when the wrapper is ≤ 5 lines and `bin/` is on sys.path[0] but `scripts/livespec/` isn't? Three options: (a) sys.path insert in wrapper, (b) `python3 -m livespec.bin.<cmd>` invocation, (c) PYTHONPATH wire-contract. Recommended (a).
turns.user: [G1 answered: option (a) — sys.path insert in wrapper.]

turns.assistant: Asked G2 (YAML front-matter parser): the three vendored libs don't parse YAML; the proposal forbids any other runtime dep. Three options: (a) hand-roll restricted YAML parser at livespec/parse/front_matter.py with format restrictions codified, (b) vendor a 4th lib like strictyaml, (c) replace YAML front-matter with inline JSON. Recommended (a).
turns.user: [G2 answered: option (a) — hand-roll restricted parser.]

turns.assistant: Asked G3 (__init__.py version check vs check-no-raise-outside-io): every exit mechanism is forbidden in __init__.py per the AST checks. Two options: (a) move version check to each bin/*.py wrapper, (b) carve out __init__.py from the AST check.
turns.user: Doesn't option A introduce duplication, though?

turns.assistant: Acknowledged the duplication concern. Proposed third option (c): extract a shared bin/_bootstrap.py module that holds sys.path setup + version check; wrapper calls bootstrap() before importing livespec. _bootstrap.py lives under bin/ so raise SystemExit is allowed by check-supervisor-discipline. This eliminates duplication AND avoids AST exemptions; wrapper shape becomes a deterministic 6-line template (also resolves G18). Re-asked G3 with shared bin/_bootstrap.py as the new recommendation.
turns.user: [G3 answered: shared bin/_bootstrap.py.]

turns.assistant: Asked G4 (pure validators load schemas from disk — contradiction): three options: (a) factory shape (validate_<name>(payload, schema) takes schema as parameter; io/ reads schemas; validate/ stays pure), (b) module-loaded with bootstrap-time read, (c) embed schemas as Python literals. Recommended (a).
turns.user: [G4 answered: option (a) — factory-shape validators.]

turns.assistant: Asked G5 (pre/post-step doctor orchestration): three options: (a) LLM drives all four steps from prose, (b) each bin/<cmd>.py subprocess-invokes bin/doctor_static.py, (c) Python commands/<cmd>.main() in-process invokes run_static. Recommended (a) initially, citing 'Python can't invoke the LLM' to argue against (b) and (c).
turns.user: I don't understand why c isn't the best choice?  Why not have all the deterministic / static logic as part of the command's ROP chain?  And we can compose the code however we want, right?  Commands can call doctor modules or ROP chains, right?

turns.assistant: Acknowledged the user was right. The post-step LLM-driven phase doesn't have to run *from inside* the wrapper — it runs *after* the wrapper exits, driven by skill prose. So all the deterministic/static lifecycle (pre-static + sub-command + post-static) can be composed as one ROP chain inside the wrapper, with the LLM only invoked for the genuinely-LLM-driven phase after the wrapper returns. That's strictly more mechanical and leans into ROP harder. Re-shaped option (c) as the new recommendation: Python wrapper's ROP chain owns pre+cmd+post static; LLM owns only the post-step LLM-driven phase. --skip-pre-check is wrapper-parsed; --skip-subjective-checks stays LLM-layer. PROPOSAL.md adds a new `Sub-command lifecycle orchestration` section.
turns.user: [G5 answered: option (c) — Python wrapper ROP chain owns pre+cmd+post static; LLM owns only LLM-driven phase.]

turns.assistant: Asked G6 (AST check semantics undefined): three options: (a) defer to first-batch propose-change, (b) spell out AST semantics in style doc now (~3-5 pages), (c) drop the AST checks entirely. Recommended (a).
turns.user: When is the mechanism we expect to use to ensure these deferred items are addressed?

turns.assistant: Honest answer: there isn't a formal mechanism today — just prose in PROPOSAL.md's Self-application section + DoD as informal acceptance gate. Both can fail silently. Proposed a stronger mechanism: seed pre-files deferred-item propose-change skeletons in SPECIFICATION/proposed_changes/ + a doctor static check + DoD item enforcing they get processed. Three options for handling G6 in light of this: (a) defer G6 + add separate G6' to formalize the deferred-items mechanism, (b) spell out AST semantics now, (c) status quo.
turns.user: if we do option one, how do we ensure those skeletons get created and we don't forget it

turns.assistant: Proposed a four-part self-enforcing mechanism: (1) registry file shipped with skill, (2) seed pre-files skeletons from registry, (3) doctor static check `deferred-items-tracked`, (4) DoD item gating v1 completion. Recursion bottoms out at the same level as every DoD item. Three options for v007: (a) add full 4-part mechanism, (b) add only doctor check + DoD item, (c) defer the full mechanism to v008.
turns.user: I think you are drifting and very confused. I am definitely confused. I thought we were just talking about how we ensure that this brainstorming of deferred tasks for the actual implementation of live spec itself gets transferred into the live spec specification once we are done brainstorming. And now it seems like you were talking about the actual seed command and its behavior in the skill. Which is a whole other topic.

turns.assistant: Apologized for drifting into proposing a runtime feature when the user's question was about the brainstorming-to-implementation transfer process. Reset: today the mechanism is informal prose (PROPOSAL.md's Self-application 'Known such items' list + DoD); items get accidentally dropped between iterations; the implementer has no surfaced 'you have N deferred items still pending.' Three honest options for the brainstorming-to-implementation transfer: (a) keep prose mechanism + add memory-rule discipline, (b) add a dedicated deferred-items.md companion doc in the brainstorming folder, (c) status quo no change.
turns.user: [Answered: option (b) — add a dedicated deferred-items.md companion doc.]

turns.assistant: Locked: deferred-items.md will be the canonical list. PROPOSAL.md's Self-application will reference it; revisions must update it; will backfill from PROPOSAL.md's current 'Known such items' plus everything accumulated this session. Returned to G6: AST-check semantics deferred to first-batch propose-change with id `ast-check-semantics`, tracked in deferred-items.md. PROPOSAL.md and style doc keep names + one-sentence purpose. Confirm or hybrid-spell-out high-risk checks?
turns.user: [G6 confirmed: defer to first-batch propose-change; tracked in deferred-items.md.]

turns.assistant: Asked G7 (CLAUDE.md coverage rule disagreement across 5 sources): two options: (a) canonicalize on scripts/ + tests/ + dev-tooling/, with _vendor/ subtree excluded; update everywhere. (b) reduce to scripts/ + tests/ only. Recommended (a).
turns.user: [G7 answered: canonicalize on scripts/ + tests/ + dev-tooling/, _vendor/ excluded.]

turns.assistant: Asked G8 (orchestrator discovery + pyright strict typing): three options: (a) Protocol + @impure_safe discovery wrapper, (b) static registry, (c) truly dynamic with # type: ignore.
turns.user: 2, static, and remove this guidance from the proposal:  'check insert/delete is a non-event' (PROPOSAL.md line 866-868).  We absolutely should hardcode the dependencies of submodules of the static python code tree

turns.assistant: Locked: static registry; v005-era 'insert/delete is a non-event' invariant dropped. The user's reasoning matches the strongest-possible-guardrails principle: hardcoded dependencies enable static type checking, mechanical enforcement that all checks are wired, and explicit enumeration is one registry edit. Will save complementary feedback memory: prefer static enumeration over dynamic discovery for skill-bundled Python.
Asked G9 (supervisor exit-code derivation undefined): two options: (a) codify the mapping in PROPOSAL.md and style doc, (b) defer to first-batch propose-change. Recommended (a).
turns.user: [G9 answered: codify in PROPOSAL.md and style doc.]

turns.assistant: Asked G10 (pyright strict + vendored libs integration): three options: (a) document integration; vendored libs touched only via typed facades under livespec/io/, (b) defer to first-batch propose-change, (c) drop Any-forbidden rule. Recommended (a).
turns.user: [G10 answered: typed facades under livespec/io/.]

turns.assistant:
Locked G10. Noted nuance: returns is special since its types are used pervasively (not just at boundaries), so the facade pattern doesn't apply uniformly to returns; either vendor the returns pyright plugin or rely on returns' own types. Captured as a deferred item: returns-pyright-plugin-disposition.

Asked G11 (propose-change orchestration ambiguity): given G5's resolution, two options: (a) rewrite propose-change/critique sections to make wrapper/LLM split explicit (bin/propose_change.py only accepts --findings-json; freeform-intent path is LLM-driven), (b) leave prose ambiguous. Recommended (a).
turns.user: [G11 answered: rewrite to make wrapper/LLM split explicit.]

turns.assistant: Asked G12 (vendor audit detail): two options: (a) add Vendoring mechanics sub-section to style doc with .vendor.toml schema + diff algorithm + just vendor-update semantics, (b) defer to first-batch propose-change. Recommended (a).
turns.user: Why do we even care about the vendoring check? Just have the specification dictate exactly what version should be vendored and leave it at that.

turns.assistant: Conceded the user's point. The check is over-engineered for the actual threat model: accidental edits caught by code review + git diff; re-vendoring at wrong version avoided by recording the version; malicious tampering has weak threat model. Three reshaped options: (a) drop check-vendor-audit; specify pinned versions + no-edit discipline; .vendor.toml records version only, (b) replace with simple immutability check (SHA-256), (c) keep full re-fetch audit. Recommended (a).
turns.user: [G12 answered: drop the check; pinned versions + no-edit discipline.]

turns.assistant:
Locked G12. Cascading edits: remove check-vendor-audit from style doc enforcement-suite list, remove dev-tooling/checks/vendor_audit.py, simplify .vendor.toml to version-recording only.

Asked G13 (bin/doctor naming asymmetry): two options: (a) add clarifying note to PROPOSAL.md ### doctor explaining that bin/doctor.py doesn't exist and the LLM follows commands/doctor.md, (b) add a bin/doctor.py wrapper that just invokes static. Recommended (a).
turns.user:
Wait a minute. I think there's something wrong here. Regarding your statement here 'The user invokes livespec doctor via the LLM (per commands/doctor.md)'
1. I thought we only had skills, not commands? Or do we have to have command wrappers so it will work via the Claude Code plugin because it doesn't auto-complete skills? Do we have anything covering this in the proposal?
2. Regardless of whether it is a command or a skill, it's still invoked as a slash command in Claude code. So if anything, the instruction would be `/livespec doctor`.  Right?

turns.assistant: Acknowledged this expanded G13 from a small naming clarification into a real gap about Claude Code skill invocation UX. Best-effort answer based on incomplete knowledge: skills are model-invoked via dialogue; user can't autocomplete a plugin-bundled skill; would need to ship slash commands at .claude-plugin/commands/<name>.md for autocomplete.
turns.user:
You are absolutely wrong here: 'Skills are model-invoked (the LLM picks up the skill autonomously based on user dialogue). The user can't autocomplete a skill — they say something like "run livespec doctor on this spec" and the LLM activates the skill.'
Skills can totally be invoked via slash commands in Claude code.  Not in other harnesses, maybe such as OpenCode.  But the actual limitation is that Claude Code can't invoke a skill as an autocomplete slash command if it lives in a plugin.
Go do research and actually understand this.

turns.assistant:
Apologized for the confidently-wrong claim. Spawned the claude-code-guide agent to research authoritatively. Agent findings: (a) skills CAN be invoked as slash commands; (b) plugin skills MUST be namespaced /<plugin>:<skill>; user was right that there's a meaningful local-vs-plugin invocation difference; (c) the commands/ subdirectory inside a skill is freeform organization, NOT a Claude Code convention; (d) NO nested subcommand syntax exists (/livespec doctor doesn't work); (e) recommended pattern for sub-command autocomplete is multiple skills (one per sub-command) with namespaced invocation. Agent cited https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills.md, https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins.md, https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins-reference.md.

Reframed G13 with grounded options: (a) restructure to one skill per sub-command (/livespec:seed, /livespec:doctor, etc.); shared scripts at plugin root, (b) keep single livespec skill; sub-commands via $ARGUMENTS, (c) hybrid. Recommended (a).
turns.user: 1 is good.  And it's also good because then the skill frontmatter can be more targeted about when it should be auto-invoked, and have granular permissions/tools.  Right?

turns.assistant:
Confirmed user's architectural point: per-sub-command skills enable tightly-scoped descriptions per skill (LLM auto-invocation matches narrow intent), allowed-tools per skill (principle of least privilege; e.g., help is read-only, prune-history is destructive), and disable-model-invocation on destructive sub-commands (must require explicit invocation). Aligns with strongest-possible-guardrails principle. Will save complementary feedback memory: prefer per-sub-command skill structure in Claude Code plugins.

Locked G13 with cascading edits: restructure skill bundle to one skill per sub-command; shared resources move to plugin root .claude-plugin/scripts/; allowed-tools and disable-model-invocation expectations documented per skill; G17 (commands/ namespace overload) dissolves naturally since the skill-side commands/ directory no longer exists.

Asked G14 (structlog bootstrap location ambiguity + check-global-writes concern): three options: (a) __init__.py + AST-check exemption documented, (b) move into shared bin/_bootstrap.py, (c) defer to first-batch. Recommended (a).
turns.user: [G14 answered: __init__.py + AST-check exemption.]

turns.assistant: Asked G15 (just check failure aggregation behavior): two options: (a) continue on failure; aggregate; non-zero if any failed (matches CI fail-fast:false), (b) stop on first failure. Recommended (a).
turns.user: [G15 answered: continue on failure; aggregate.]

turns.assistant: Asked G16 (lefthook install missing from bootstrap sequence): two options: (a) add `just bootstrap` target that runs `lefthook install` + any other one-time setup, (b) document as separate manual step. Recommended (a).
turns.user: [G16 answered: add `just bootstrap` target.]

turns.assistant: Asked G17 + G18 confirmation: both already resolved by earlier dispositions (G17 by G13's restructure dissolving the skill-side commands/ directory; G18 by G3's shared _bootstrap.py establishing the 6-line deterministic wrapper shape). Three options: (a) confirm both as cascading and proceed to dogfood lifecycle, (b) re-open G17 to rename livespec.commands package, (c) re-open G18 to specify a different wrapper shape. Recommended (a).
turns.user: [G17 + G18 confirmed as cascading. Proceed to dogfood lifecycle.]

turns.assistant: Summarized all 18 dispositions in a table. Listed three feedback memories to save: (1) prefer static enumeration over dynamic discovery for skill-bundled Python, (2) prefer per-sub-command skill structure in Claude Code plugins for tightly-scoped frontmatter and granular permissions, (3) each brainstorming revision MUST enumerate carried-forward deferred items. Asked whether ready to proceed to dogfood lifecycle or revisit anything first.
turns.user: [Proceed to dogfood lifecycle.]

turns.assistant: Executed dogfood lifecycle: wrote history/v007/proposed_changes/proposal-critique-v06-revision.md (per-item dispositions, governing principles, outstanding follow-ups, what-was-rejected). Wrote brainstorming/deferred-items.md as the new canonical tracking list with discipline + item schema + 8 entries (5 carried forward from v006: template-prompt-authoring, python-style-doc-into-constraints, companion-docs-mapping, enforcement-check-scripts, claude-md-prose, task-runner-and-ci-config; 3 new from v007: ast-check-semantics, returns-pyright-plugin-disposition, front-matter-parser). Rewrote PROPOSAL.md to v007 incorporating all 18 dispositions: per-sub-command skill restructure, sub-command lifecycle orchestration section, shared bin/_bootstrap.py + 6-line wrapper shape, static registry for doctor checks, factory-shape validators, exit-code derivation codified, drop check-vendor-audit, CLAUDE.md scope canonicalized, just bootstrap target, structlog bootstrap, propose-change/critique LLM-vs-wrapper split made explicit, no-bin/doctor.py asymmetry documented. Updated python-skill-script-style-requirements.md to match: shared _bootstrap.py contract, 6-line wrapper shape, factory-shape validators, static registry, vendored-lib facades, drop vendor-audit, CLAUDE.md scope, just bootstrap, structlog exemption. Copied PROPOSAL.md to history/v007/PROPOSAL.md byte-identical (verified). Moved critique from brainstorming/proposed_changes/v007-proposed-change-proposal-critique-v06.md to history/v007/proposed_changes/proposal-critique-v06.md. Updated history/README.md with v007 entry (initially placed in wrong chronological order; fixed via Python script to ensure v001→v002→...→v006→v007 order); updated final Pointer paragraph. Wrote this conversation.json. Saved three feedback memories.
